sections.paragraph:
(with prefix and suffix לֶאֱלֹהַּ Dan 11:38 לֵאלֹהֹו Hab 1:11), m. God (Arab. إِلَاهُ, إِلَهُ, with art. أَلَّلهُ of the true God; Syr. ܠܰܠܳܗܐܳ; Chald. אֱלָהּ). In imitation of the Aramean usage, the singular form is only used in poetry and in the later Hebrew; the plural of majesty, אֱלֹהִים, occurs, on the other hand, more than two thousand times. The singular is used-

(1) of any god, -Dan. 11:3739 ; 2Ch 32:15 Neh 9:17. There is a proverbial expression, Hab 1:11, of an obstinate self-confident man, זוּ כֹתֹו לֵאלֹהֹו “whose own strength is as his god,” i.e. who despises every god and confides in his own strong hand and sword. Comp. Job 12:6, אֲשֶׁר הֵבִיא אֱלֹוהַּ בְּיָדֹו “who bears his god in his hand.” Arms are intended. Comp. Virg. Æn. vi. 773, “Dextra, mihi deus, et telum … Nunc adsint.”

(2) mostly of the true God, κατʼ ἐξοχήν, for הָאֱלֹוהַּ, أَللَّهُ. Deu 32:15 Psa 50:22, and forty times in the book of Job. Const. with sing. adj. (Deu. loc. cit.) and plur. Job 35:10.

Pl. אֱלֹהִים (with pref. contr. בֵּאלֹהִים, בֵּאלֹהים, לֵאלֹהִים) used in Hebrew-

(A) in a plural sense-

(1) of gods or deities in general, whether true or false. אֱלֹהֵי מִצְרַיִם “the gods of the Egyptians,” Exo 12:12. אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר “strange gods,” Gen 35:2, Gen 35:4 Deu 29:18. אֱלֹהִים חֲדָשִׁים “new gods,” Deu 32:17. Sometimes, from the more common popular usage, Jehovah and idols are comprehended under this common name; Psa 86:8, “there is none like unto thee among the gods, O Jehovah!” Exo 18:11 22:19 . Elsewhere the idea of divinity is altogether denied to idols, and is attributed to Jehovah alone. Isa 44:6, “besides me there is no god;” Isa 45:5, 14 Isa 45:14, 21 Isa 45:21 46:9 . Idols are even called לֹא־אֱלֹהִים 2Ch 13:9.

(2) once applied to kings, i.q. בְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים Psa 82:1, especially verse Psa 82:6.

Note. Not a few interpreters, both ancient and modern, have regarded אֱלֹהִים as also denoting angels (see Psa 8:6, the LXX. and Ch.; Psa 82:1 97:7 138:1 ), and judges ( Exo 21:6 22:7 , Exo 22:8); this opinion is discussed and refuted at length in Thes. page 95. [But Hebrews, chaps. 1:6 and 2:7 , shew plainly that this word sometimes means angels, and the authority of the N.T. decides the matter.]

(B) in a singular sense, of one god (compare as to the pl. majestatis or excellentiœ, Lehrg. page 663, 664), Heb. Gram. § 106, 2, b. Constr. with a verb ( Gen 1:1, seq.) and adjective in the singular, as אֱלֹהִים חַי 2Ki 19:4, 16 2Ki 19:16אֱלֹהִים צַדִּיק Psa 7:10 57:3 78:56 but with a plural verb only in certain phrases. Perhaps retained from polytheism [an idea which is not to be entertained for a moment], in which אֱלֹהִים may be taken in a plural sense and understood of higher powers. [This is not the way in which the Scripture speaks of God.] Gen 20:13, הִחְעוּ אֹחִי אֱלֹהִים as if, “gods made me wander;” Gen 35:7 Exo 22:8 32:4 , Exo 32:8 2Sa 7:23 1Ki 19:2 Ps. 58:12 . Compare my Comment. de Pent. Sam. page 58. It is used also-

(1) of any divinity. Deu 32:39, “there is no god beside me;” Psa 14:1. Thus, when the divine nature is opposed to the human. Eze 28:2 Psa 8:6, “thou madest him a little lower than God” [than the angels, see Heb 2:7 ]. Very often-

(2) of an idol, a god of the Gentiles. Exo 32:1, “make us a god,” i.e. an idol; 1Sa 5:7, “Dagon, our god;” 2Ki 1:2, 2Ki 1:3, 2Ki 1:6, 16 2Ki 1:16. Even used of a goddess, 1Ki 11:5.

(3) the god of any one is the god whom any one worships, whom he has as his domestic god, ἐπιχώριος, tutelar. Jon 1:5, “every one called upon his god;” Rth 1:16 Gen 17:7, Gen 17:8 28:21 . Thus, the God of the Israelites is Jehovah, who is thus very often called אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵלּ Exo 5:1 Psa. 41:14 אֱלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב Psa 20:2 46:8 and conjoinedly יְהֹוָה אֱלֹהַי Psa 18:29 יְהֹוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ in Deuteronomy more than two hundred times.

(4) more rarely followed by a genitive of that over which the god presides, or that which he created, just as Mars is called the god of war [No such comparison ought to be made of the true God with phrases relating to idols.], ex. gr. אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וְהָאָרֶץ Gen 24:3 אֱלֹהֵי הַצְּבָאֹוח “God of the heavenly hosts,” Amo 3:13 or the attribute of God as אֱלֹהֵי אָמֵן “God of truth,” Isa 65:16.

(5) אֱלֹהִים is used for a divine, godlike appearance or form [?] (Götter=, Geiftergeftalt), 1Sa 28:13 where the sorceress says to Saul, “I see a godlike form arising from the earth.”

(6) with the art. הָאֱלֹהִים is GOD, κατʼ ἐξοχήν, the one and true God; Arab. أَللَّهُ, in the well-known phrase لاَ إَِلَهَ إِلَّاآلَّلهُ. Deu 4:35, כִּי יְהֹוָה הוּא הָאֱלֹהִים “for Jehovah is the (true) God;” 1Ki 18:21, “if Jehovah is (God), follow him, if Baal is (God), follow him.” Verse 37 1Ki 18:37 Deu 7:9. Whence הָאֱלֹהִים is used very often of Jehovah, Gen 5:22 6:9 , 11 17:18 20:6 , Gen 20:7, etc. But equivalent to this is אֱלֹהִים without the article ( Jos 22:34), which is used very often both in prose and in poetry, with hardly any distinction, for יְהֹוָה, either so that both names are employed together, or the use of the one or the other depends on the nature of the phrases and the usage of the language, and the inclination of the particular writers. Thus we constantly find בְּנֵי אֱלֹּהִים, and on the other hand, מְשִׁיחַ יְהֹוָה, נְאֻם יְהֹוָה; in other expressions this use is altogether promiscuous, as עֶבֶד יְהֹוָה and עֶבֶד הָאֱלֹהִים Dan 9:11 רוּתַ יְהֹוָה and רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים Gen 1:2 41:38 Exo 31:3. As to the usage of different writers, see the remarks in Thes. page 97, 98.

Things are said in Scripture to be of God; whatever is most excellent or distinguished in its own kind was regarded by the ancients as specially proceeding from God, or sent, or created by him, or what bears a divine or august appearance (τὸ θεῖον), as “mount of God,” Psa 68:16 “river of God,” Psa 65:10 תִתַּת אֱלֹהִים “terror suddenly sent by God,” a panic terror, Gen 35:5. 1Sa 14:15, אֵשׁ אֱלֹהִים is used of lightning, etc.; compare אֵל page 45.B . Similar is the principle of the phrase לֵאלֹּהִים of God; Gr. τῷ Θεῷ, added to adjectives. Jon 3:3, עִיר גְּדֹולָה לֵאלֹּהִים pr. “a city divinely great.” Act 7:20, ἀστεῖος τῷ Θεῷ. Compare the Arab. لِلَّهِ pr. from God, divinely, exceedingly. Har. Cons. iv. page 38, ed. de Sacy.

As to the phrases, אִישׁ אֱלֹחִים, בֶּן אֱלֹהִים see under אִישׁ , בֵּן and the other words from which they spring.

Note. Some regard אֱלֹהִים to be also used in a singular sense (for as to the plural see A, 2 ), of one King for בֶּן־אֱלֹהִים, and they especially refer to Psa 45:7, where they render כִּםְאֲךָ אֱלֹהִים עֹולָם וָעֶד “thy throne, O God (i.e. O divine King), shall stand for ever;” but this should no doubt [?] be construed by ellipsis, כִּסְאֲךָ כִּסֵּא אֱלֹהִים “thy throne shall be a divine throne” (i.e. guarded and made prosperous by God), according to the accustomed canon of the language, Lehrg. § 233:6. [This passage speaks of Christ as God, there is no ellipsis to be supplied, see Heb 1:8.]
